title_en: The Beatific Vision — Seeing Allah in the Hereafter
title_ar: The Beatific Vision — Seeing Allah in the Hereafter
content_id:
Ru'yatullah — melihat Allah oleh orang-orang beriman di akhirat — termasuk doktrin paling agung dalam teologi Islam dan mewakili puncak balasan bagi penghuni surga. Al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah menegaskan doktrin ini secara jelas berdasarkan ayat-ayat Al-Qur'an yang tegas dan hadis-hadis mutawatir, dan dengan melakukan ini, ia membedakan Ahl al-Sunnah dari mereka — khususnya kaum Mu'tazilah — yang mengingkari kemungkinan tersebut.

Al-Tahawi menyatakan: 'Ru'yatullah adalah kenyataan bagi penghuni surga, tanpa mereka meliputi-Nya dan tanpa menyebutkan caranya (bila kayf).' Penegasan ini langsung diambil dari ayat Al-Qur'an: 'Wajah-wajah pada hari itu berseri-seri, melihat kepada Tuhannya' (QS. al-Qiyamah: 22-23). Ungkapan 'melihat kepada Tuhannya' (ila rabbiha nadzirah) tidak ambigu secara gramatikal maupun semantik dalam bahasa Arab — ia menggambarkan persepsi visual langsung terhadap Allah. Kaum Mu'tazilah, enggan menerima apa yang mereka anggap sebagai implikasi antropomorfik, menafsirkan kembali 'nadzirah' sebagai 'menunggu' (dari akar kata Arab yang sama) — suatu pembacaan yang dianggap dipaksakan dan tidak meyakinkan oleh para ahli bahasa klasik.

Doktrin ini diperkuat lebih lanjut oleh hadis masyhur yang diriwayatkan dalam Shahih al-Bukhari dan Muslim, di mana Nabi shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam bersabda: 'Kalian akan melihat Tuhan kalian sebagaimana kalian melihat bulan purnama — kalian tidak akan berdesakan dalam memandang-Nya.' Perbandingan ini bukan perbandingan keserupaan (bulan tidak seperti Allah) tetapi perbandingan kejelasan dan kepastian — sebagaimana bulan purnama tampak jelas tanpa keraguan atau ambiguitas, demikian pula orang-orang beriman akan melihat Tuhan mereka. Perbandingan ini berfungsi untuk menegaskan realitas dan kejelasan penglihatan sambil meninggalkan sepenuhnya hakikat dari apa yang dilihat.

Pendirian Ahl al-Sunnah, sebagaimana diringkas al-Tahawi, adalah bahwa penglihatan ini nyata, terjadi tanpa pemahaman para mukmin meliputi Allah (la yuhithuna bihi 'ilman — 'ilmu tidak meliputi-Nya,' QS. Thaha: 110), dan terjadi 'tanpa menyebutkan cara' (bila kayf) — artinya kita menegaskan realitasnya tanpa mengklaim memahami modalitas atau mekanismenya. Allah dilihat — secara benar dan langsung — tanpa mengimplikasikan bahwa Dia adalah tubuh, memiliki arah, menempati ruang, atau serupa dengan ciptaan-Nya dengan cara apa pun.

Untuk pembahasan teologis, al-Tahawi juga secara implisit membahas perbedaan antara dunia ini dan akhirat. Di dunia ini, penglihatan terhadap Allah tidak diberikan — bahkan Musa pun ditolak permohonan penglihatan langsung yang ia minta (QS. al-A'raf: 143), meskipun ia diberi sebagian pengungkapan yang mengakibatkan dirinya pingsan. Di akhirat, setelah pemurnian Hari Kebangkitan dan penyeberangan Shirat, anugerah tertinggi ini diberikan kepada penghuni surga sebagai kebahagiaan terbesar mereka — melebihi surga itu sendiri dalam pandangan para ulama, berdasarkan hadis yang menggambarkan sabda Nabi shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam tentang kehormatan tertinggi ini.

Penegasan Ru'yatullah dengan demikian bukan sekadar proposisi teologis tetapi sumber motivasi spiritual yang mendalam: seorang Muslim beribadah, berjuang, dan bersabar di dunia ini, dengan harapan akhirnya dapat melihat — dengan mata kepala sendiri — Dzat yang selama ini mereka ibadahi sepanjang hidup mereka.
content_en:
The Beatific Vision — the seeing of Allah (ru'yat Allah) by the believers in the Hereafter — is among the most sublime doctrines of Islamic theology and represents the pinnacle of reward for the people of Paradise. Al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah affirms this doctrine clearly on the basis of explicit Quranic verses and mass-transmitted hadiths, and in doing so, it distinguishes Ahl al-Sunnah from those — particularly the Mu'tazilah — who denied this possibility.

Al-Tahawi states: 'The Beatific Vision is a reality for the people of Paradise, without their encompassing Him and without specifying how (bila kayf).' This affirmation follows directly from the Quranic verse: 'Some faces that Day will be radiant, looking at their Lord' (Quran 75:22-23). The phrase 'looking at their Lord' (ila rabbiha nazirah) is grammatically and semantically unambiguous in Arabic — it describes a direct visual perception of Allah. The Mu'tazilah, unwilling to accept what they considered an anthropomorphic implication, reinterpreted 'nazirah' as 'waiting' (from the same Arabic root) — a reading that classical grammarians have considered forced and unconvincing.

The doctrine is further grounded in the famous hadith recorded in both al-Bukhari and Muslim, in which the Prophet ﷺ said: 'You shall see your Lord as you see the full moon — you will not be crowded in your vision of Him.' This comparison is not one of likeness (the moon is not like Allah) but of clarity and certainty — just as the full moon is visible without doubt or ambiguity, so shall the believers see their Lord. The comparison serves to affirm the reality and clarity of the vision while leaving entirely open the nature of what is seen.

The position of Ahl al-Sunnah, as al-Tahawi encapsulates it, is that this vision is real, that it occurs without the believers' comprehension encompassing Allah (la yuhituna bihi ilman — 'knowledge does not encompass Him,' Quran 20:110), and that it occurs 'without specifying how' (bila kayf) — meaning we affirm the reality without claiming to understand its modality or mechanism. Allah is seen — truly and directly — without this implying that He is a body, has a direction, occupies space, or is in any way like His creation.

For the theological discussion, al-Tahawi also implicitly addresses the difference between this world and the next. In this world, vision of Allah is not granted — even Musa was refused the direct vision he sought (Quran 7:143), though he was given a partial disclosure that resulted in his fainting. In the Hereafter, after the purification of the Resurrection and the crossing of the Sirat, this ultimate gift is bestowed upon the people of Paradise as their greatest joy — surpassing even Paradise itself in the estimation of the scholars, based on the hadith that describes the Prophet's ﷺ words about this supreme honor.

The affirmation of the Beatific Vision is thus not merely a theological proposition but a source of profound spiritual motivation: the Muslim worships, strives, and endures in this world, with the hope of ultimately seeing — with their own eyes — the One they have worshipped throughout their lives.
content_ar:
The Beatific Vision — the seeing of الله (ru'yat الله) by the believers in the Hereafter — is among the most sublime doctrines of Islamic theology and represents the pinnacle of reward for the people of Paradise. Al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah affirms this doctrine clearly on the basis of explicit Quranic verses and mass-transmitted hadiths, and in doing so, it distinguishes Ahl al-Sunnah from those — particularly the Mu'tazilah — who denied this possibility.

Al-Tahawi states: 'The Beatific Vision is a reality for the people of Paradise, without their encompassing Him and without specifying how (bila kayf).' This affirmation follows directly from the Quranic verse: 'Some faces that Day will be radiant, looking at their Lord' (Quran 75:22-23). The phrase 'looking at their Lord' (ila rabbiha nazirah) is grammatically and semantically unambiguous in Arabic — it describes a direct visual perception of الله. The Mu'tazilah, unwilling to accept what they considered an anthropomorphic implication, reinterpreted 'nazirah' as 'waiting' (from the same Arabic root) — a reading that classical grammarians have considered forced and unconvincing.

The doctrine is further grounded in the famous hadith recorded in both al-Bukhari and Muslim, in which النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم صلى الله عليه وسلم said: 'You shall see your Lord as you see the full moon — you will not be crowded in your vision of Him.' This comparison is not one of likeness (the moon is not like الله) but of clarity and certainty — just as the full moon is visible without doubt or ambiguity, so shall the believers see their Lord. The comparison serves to affirm the reality and clarity of the vision while leaving entirely open the nature of what is seen.

The position of Ahl al-Sunnah, as al-Tahawi encapsulates it, is that this vision is real, that it occurs without the believers' comprehension encompassing الله (la yuhituna bihi ilman — 'knowledge does not encompass Him,' Quran 20:110), and that it occurs 'without specifying how' (bila kayf) — meaning we affirm the reality without claiming to understand its modality or mechanism. الله is seen — truly and directly — without this implying that He is a body, has a direction, occupies space, or is in any way like His creation.

For the theological discussion, al-Tahawi also implicitly addresses the difference between this world and the next. In this world, vision of الله is not granted — even Musa was refused the direct vision he sought (Quran 7:143), though he was given a partial disclosure that resulted in his fainting. In the Hereafter, after the purification of the Resurrection and the crossing of the Sirat, this ultimate gift is bestowed upon the people of Paradise as their greatest joy — surpassing even Paradise itself in the estimation of the scholars, based on the hadith that describes النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم's صلى الله عليه وسلم words about this supreme honor.

The affirmation of the Beatific Vision is thus not merely a theological proposition but a source of profound
